Thank God they didn’t mess it up like they did with Superman but that's what you expect from DC movies. The Fantastic Four finally feels like it's in the right hands. What really sets it apart is that beautiful vintage touch — it gives the film a unique identity that stands out from the typical MCU formula. It feels like a respectful nod to the classic comics while still offering something fresh. If Marvel keeps this tone consistent, this might actually be the Fantastic Four film we've been waiting for.

A cinematic symphony of visuals, emotional stakes, and familial heart. It’s smart, stylish, and deeply human—The Fantastic Four: First Steps doesn’t just reboot a franchise; it redefines what modern superhero storytelling can be. In IMAX, it’s not just watched—it’s felt, epic and intimate at once.

2025 hasn’t seen anything more triumphant than this. It commands all 10 stars—the best superhero film of the year, deserving of every ounce of praise

I just watched the new version of Fantastic Four, and it completely ruined my childhood memories. The movie was utterly pathetic — terrible direction and even worse writing. Please do not waste your time and money on this film.

Anyone who has seen the original Fantastic Four will understand what I mean. The older version had its charm, but this new one is just disappointing. After watching it, I truly feel that Hollywood has lost its touch when it comes to making good superhero movies.

This film has no real story, no purpose — it’s pure nonsense. If you’re looking to waste your time and ruin your fond memories, then go ahead and watch it. Otherwise, steer clear. It’s a total disaster of a movie.

Best Marvel movie since Infinity War. This movie actually had a soul. It wasn't made just for the sake of releasing a movie, or making money. The script, the casting, the art direction, the cinematography, was DRIPPING with passion and love. If you're watching the trailer trying to decide if you want to see the movie, and you see this comment... I want you to know that this isn't just a comic book movie, it's a film. It's exactly what I want out of a comic book movie. It was made with love and care, it cares about the source material, and it isn't afraid to be what it is. A Fantastic Four film. It's like you've dunked your head INTO a comic book.

Fantastic solid movie. Easy 9/10, but could have been 10/10 if it were a bit longer.

Slight spoilers for the movie:






One of the best touches I loved about this movie is that the Four are not just their powers.
Sue is the founder of the Future Foundation. She works to broker peace with enemies.
Johnny is the heart of the group, and his emotional intelligence covers the gaps in Reed’s logic.
Ben is a fantastic pilot. Anakin would be proud.
And it was so easy to actually _believe_ that Reed was the smartest man alive. Everything had to be something he thought of. His work is everywhere. He leaves thumbprints on every piece of technology, his logic wrapped around him like steel. Dark thoughts enter his head, but he has enough love for his family to counter them. But they win half the time.

The thing is that I truly loved the original film regardless of its flaws. I was a huge fan of the comic book movies of that era. But I despised them for the last decade or so. That is not to say that there have not been exceptions. Let me put it this way: when I used to see a comic book movie trailer in the early 2000s I got so excited and was happy to go to the theatres. Now, I can hardly even bring myself to watch the trailer and have not been to the theatres in several years.

Most of the time, I get a good feel for if a movie will be bad based on the trailer. But it is possible to have a good trailer and a bad movie. I hope that this movie will be one of the exceptions. We will see.

P.S. It is also possible for a good movie to have a bad trailer.

Jeff has a theory. In the trailer, a baby is briefly shown wearing a fantastic four onesie, indicating that it is most definitely the baby of Mr Fantastic and the Invisible Woman. In the comics, they have two children: Franklin and Valeria. Valeria went on to become the smartest person in the multiverse, even surpassing her father. Their other child was named Franklin, who was essentially a cosmic entity because of how powerful he is. He even used his powers to create and shape entire universes, with the help of the molecule man. At the end of “Fantastic Four: Fourever”, he even states that nothing on earth is really worthy of his power and that cosmic beings like annihalus are the only people he will fight, as his power decreases everytime he uses it. I think that, when galactus is about to consume earth in the climax of the movie, the baby, (presumably Franklin), will use his powers to kill or at least stop galactus. But that’s just a theory. A film theory.

At 25, most are still figuring it out.

But Shreyash Mahalle, known online as Shreylord, has already built what many only dream of — a multi-brand consumer ecosystem designed to shape the future of how India eats, heals and lives.

He is the Founder and CEO of Leap Bombay, a parent company behind brands like VANYA, Born Again, and MediPure. Each of these ventures reflects something deeper than just business. They are systems. Built with intention. Structured with silence. Scaled through obsession.

Shreyash did not follow the traditional founder path.
In 2016, he nearly lost his life. That moment changed everything.

With no direction but a deep hunger to understand the world, he immersed himself in audience psychology, product behavior, and the business world. He earned his BSc in Biology while studying entrepreneurship in silence. During COVID, he faced long periods of depression that forced him to look inward. That inner journey sparked a new level of self-awareness, influenced heavily by Osho, spiritual practice, and ancient wisdom.

He then completed his MBBS at AIIMS Jodhpur — one of the top medical institutes in India — and chose not to follow the medical route but instead to build the future of health and wellness outside the clinic.

From starting as a musician with millions of organic streams to mastering YouTube content systems, running multi-platform pages, and consulting on brand psychology, he now leads one of India’s most promising emerging brand houses.

VANYA focuses on 100 percent natural jungle-sourced food
Born Again is a biohacking and anti-aging skincare brand
MediPure is a clean hygiene brand for conscious homes and institutions

All ventures are completely bootstrapped.
Shreyash is now selectively opening Leap Bombay to aligned venture capital and strategic investors who share his decade-long vision for building not just products, but new categories

He has been featured in podcasts, digital news, and business magazines for his unique approach to brand-building and audience-first ecosystems

His core belief remains simple
I will never give up

Shreyash Mahalle is not trying to be everywhere
But what he is building quietly is something that very soon
Everyone will see

Connect with him or explore Leap Bombay if you believe in building slow, deep, and right

"It's clobberin' time" is this phases "Avengers, assemble". They'll milk it until the end of the movie, he'll say it once, and then you won't hear it again until this phase's Endgame movie. Marvel have their formula, and they've gotten to big to risk straying too far from it. The last time they did stray from the formula was Guardians of the Galaxy, and the guy who made that work is now in control of their direct competitors creative vision. I want to see F4 succeed on screen. I grew up on those comics. But I don't see anything different here from what we've had before from F4 movies or Marvel, other than better CGI. The most interesting thing about this is the time period it seems to be set in. I hope the rest of the MCU going forward is set in this 60s futurist time. It's going to make X-Men a lot better. I just think Superman is going to be a much better movie overall. I'll be very happy to be proven wrong though.

I like the tone.  I don't like the whole "Clobberin' Time" thing.  It seems like a joke that they don't know how to wrap up, or that goes on too long.

I like the shot near the end where we see Mr. Fantastic use his stretching abilities, and see that it's not crazy rubbery, but more low key and subtle.  I like the vintage 60s aesthetic with those old timey cars.
I actually like the way Silver Surfette looks in the shots she appeared in.  I don't like her little "I herald his beginning" monologue.  It's too dramatic, and a serious character would never be so cliche.

I also don't like Johnny Storm yelling "woohoo!" when he's flying as Human Torch.  It's as if it's the first time he's ever used his flying powers, and it's earth-shattering to him.  
Kind of the same as Spider-Man swinging.  Like, the first time ever?  Yeah, I can see that.  But not every time.  That's kind of ridiculous.

Overall, I do like the tone of it.  It's brighter and more colorful, especially coming after Thunderbolts*.

I'm curious to know how it'll do.
